reasoning: Volatility indices like the CBOE Crude Oil ETF Volatility Index (OVX) typically display negatively skewed changes over time—characterized by rapid, brief spikes followed by long, slow periods of decay back to their baseline. The median return over a random interval is usually negative, meaning that the probability of the index being higher on a future date from an arbitrary start point is typically slightly below 50%. A known recent value of 37.90 is somewhat elevated compared to historical quiet-period medians, reinforcing the likelihood of volatility decay. Thus, for a short-term forecast (like one week), the probability of the index closing higher is less than 0.5. As the forecast horizon extends to 1 month and 3 months, it tends more towards mean-reverting unforecastable long-term variance. Adhering to the prompt's explicit instruction that uncertainty must increase with the forecast horizon (i.e., probabilities for more distant dates should move closer to 0.5), I set the short-term probability at 46%, moving to 48% at the one-month horizon, and 49% at the three-month horizon.
